skill.name: test-driven-development
skill.description: Guide AI coding agents to use strict test-driven development before implementing production changes.
skill.summary: Enforces a red-green-refactor workflow for features, bug fixes, and refactoring.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples and TDD guidance rather than executable skill code. The only confirmed risk factor is local test-command guidance, such as npm test, which is expected for a coding workflow and does not show exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Local Test Command Guidance
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for external command guidance. The skill tells the agent to run local project tests, including npm test, but the commands are explicit, user-visible, and limited to normal verification steps.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain local test-runner examples. I found no dynamic shell construction, remote download, credential access, or data exfiltration around these commands.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for Ruby or shell backtick execution. The flagged locations are fenced Markdown blocks, TypeScript examples, tables, or command examples in documentation, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both files are Markdown references and the suspicious tokens occur inside documentation examples. No script file, command wrapper, or runtime hook is present in the scanned file set.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptographic algorithm findings. The cited lines discuss TDD wording, ordinary test examples, or cleanup helpers and do not implement MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited locations found no cryptographic API usage. The matches appear to be substring or context-free scanner noise in prose and examples.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for system or network reconnaissance. The flagged locations mention reading a local reference, passing tests, or avoiding excessive mocking, not host discovery or network scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain testing guidance only. No commands such as port scans, host enumeration, interface discovery, or network requests appear in context.

content.value_statement: Teams often ship code without proof that the tests catch real failures. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through strict red-green-refactor work.
actual_capabilities: Requires a failing test before production implementation work begins.
Guides red-green-refactor cycles for features, bug fixes, and refactoring.
Checks that tests fail for the expected reason before code is written.
Promotes minimal implementation before cleanup and design improvement.
Identifies testing anti-patterns such as testing mocks or adding test-only production methods.
Provides verification checklists before marking coding work complete.
limitations: Does not provide a test framework adapter or runnable automation script.
Assumes the project already has a working test command or test setup.
May be too strict for throwaway prototypes or generated code.
Does not replace human judgment for unusual testing constraints.

use_cases.title: Implement a New Feature Safely
use_cases.description: Write the expected behavior as a failing test, then implement only enough code to pass it.

use_cases.title: Fix a Regression With Proof
use_cases.description: Capture the bug in a failing test before changing the production code that caused it.

use_cases.title: Review Test Quality
use_cases.description: Check whether tests verify real behavior instead of mocks, setup details, or implementation trivia.

prompt_templates.title: Start a TDD Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the test-driven-development skill for this feature. First identify one behavior, then write the smallest failing test before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Bug With Red Green Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use strict TDD for this bug. Reproduce the bug with a failing test, confirm the failure reason, then implement the smallest fix.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Without Behavior Drift
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the TDD workflow to this refactor. Confirm current behavior with tests before changes, then keep the suite green after each cleanup step.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Test Anti-Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the testing anti-patterns reference to review these tests. Identify mock-focused assertions, test-only production APIs, and incomplete test doubles.

output_examples.input: Add retry behavior for failed operations.
output: A first failing test describes the expected retry count and final result.
The implementation stays minimal until the test passes.
Refactoring happens only after the behavior is covered and green.

output_examples.input: Fix a form bug where an empty email is accepted.
output: A regression test captures the empty-email failure before code changes.
The fix adds only the validation needed for that case.
The final check confirms the new test and existing suite pass.

output_examples.input: Review tests that mock a sidebar component.
output: The review separates real page behavior from mock existence checks.
Assertions move toward user-visible navigation behavior.
Mocks are kept only where they preserve the behavior under test.

best_practices: Watch each new test fail for the expected reason before implementation.
Implement the smallest production change that makes the failing test pass.
Refactor only after the relevant test and surrounding suite are green.
anti_patterns: Writing production code first and adding tests afterward.
Testing that mocks exist instead of testing real behavior.
Adding production methods that exist only to help tests.

faq.question: Does this skill write tests for me?
faq.answer: It guides the agent to write tests first and verify failures before implementation.

faq.question: Can it work with any test framework?
faq.answer: Yes, it is framework-agnostic. The project still needs its own working test command.

faq.question: Is this useful for bug fixes?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks for a failing regression test before changing the faulty code.

faq.question: Does it allow mocks?
faq.answer: Yes, but it discourages testing mock behavior or hiding important side effects.

faq.question: When should I skip this skill?
faq.answer: Consider skipping only for throwaway prototypes, generated code, or configuration-only changes.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents local test commands that the agent may use during normal coding verification.
